title: Lab 02 — Standard Errors and Randomization Tests
text:
Load libraries
Load tidyverse library

library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.2.1     ✔ readr     2.2.0
✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
✔ ggplot2   4.0.3     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
✔ purrr     1.2.2     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

library(here) # resolves files from the project root regardless of where qmd lives

here() starts at /mnt/data1/documents/ISU/RProjects/STAT5101




Writing functions in R
Load data (user header=TRUE)

creativity <- read.csv(here("data/creativity.csv"), header = TRUE)
head(creativity)

  treatment score
1 intrinsic  12.0
2 intrinsic  12.0
3 intrinsic  12.9
4 intrinsic  13.6
5 intrinsic  16.6
6 intrinsic  17.2


Define function for computing the standard error

se <- function(x) {
    s <- sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
    n <- sum(!is.na(x))
    s / sqrt(n)
}

Calculate standard error for all 47 observation scores (just the score column)

se(creativity$score)

[1] 0.7653046


Using tidyverse, summarize the standard error (a different way to calculate)

creativity |>
    summarize(
        se = se(score)
    )

         se
1 0.7653046


Group by treatment and compute the standard error by treatment group

creativity |>
    group_by(treatment) |>
    summarize(
        se = se(score)
    )

# A tibble: 2 × 2
  treatment    se
  <chr>     <dbl>
1 extrinsic 1.10 
2 intrinsic 0.906




Randomization Tests
First set seed for reproducibility

set.seed(5101)

Calculate the observed difference in mean score (observed means as documented in creativity.csv)

observed_diff <- creativity |>
    group_by(treatment) |>
    summarize(mean_score = mean(score)) |>
    summarize(diff = diff(mean_score)) |>
    pull(diff)

observed_diff

[1] 4.100725


Run randomization (basically, simulate what else, other than what was recorded in the csv, could have been observed at random)

nsim <- 10000
simulated_diffs <- replicate(nsim, {
    # Everthing in {} is evaluated as if it was a function (this is like a lambda)
    creativity |>
        # generate a random treatment value
        mutate(shuffled = sample(treatment)) |>
        # group by the random treatment value
        group_by(shuffled) |>
        # Calculate the mean and diff like before
        summarize(mean_score = mean(score)) |>
        summarize(diff = diff(mean_score)) |>
        # pull the difference
        pull(diff)
})
head(simulated_diffs)

[1]  2.7043478  1.4356884  0.9077899 -1.6465580  0.8141304  1.8614130


Calculate the p-value

p_value <- (1 + sum(abs(simulated_diffs) >= abs(observed_diff))) / (nsim + 1)
p_value

[1] 0.00489951


Explanation of p-value calculation: The p-value is the number of observations with a value equal to or more extreme (>=) than the observed value divided by the number of observations. This would translate to:
sum(abs(simulated_diffs) >= abs(simulated_diff)) / nsim
But, this results in a 0 p-value if by chance the observed_diff was an extreme value itself and none of the simulated values were equal or larger resulting in 0 / nsim = 0, which is an impossible p-value.
By adding 1 to both the numerator and denominator, essentially, we are including the observed_diff in the calculation. Since observed_diff is equal to itself, it is valid to include it as a count in the numerator and increment the denominator by 1 as if we performed 10001 simulations instead of 10000.


Self Assessment
Read in the tomato data, tomato.csv

tomato <- read.csv(here("data/tomato.csv"), header = TRUE)
head(tomato)

  treatment yield
1         a  0.39
2         b  0.44
3         a  1.83
4         a  5.66
5         b  0.33
6         a  8.06



(a) Draw side-by-side box plots of the yield for each fertilizer

ggplot(data = tomato, mapping=aes(x = treatment, y = yield)) +
    geom_boxplot()










(b) Estimate the difference in mean yield between the two fertilizers (a-b)

observed_diff = tomato |>
    group_by(treatment) |>
    summarize(mean_yield = mean(yield)) |>
    summarize(diff = diff(mean_yield)) |>
    pull(diff)
observed_diff

[1] -6.531429




(c) Estimate the difference in median yield

tomato |>
    group_by(treatment) |>
    summarize(median_yield = median(yield)) |>
    summarize(diff = diff(median_yield)) |>
    pull(diff)

[1] -4.64




(d) Use a randomization test to test the null hypothesis of no effect of the fertilizer on mean yield. Report the two-sided p-value
Run randomization test and collect simulated diffs

# Set seed for reproducibility
set.seed(5101)

# We have already calculated the observed diff above
# Run randomization test

nsim <- 10000
simulated_diffs <- replicate(nsim, {
    tomato |>
        mutate(shuffled = sample(treatment)) |>
        group_by(shuffled) |>
        summarize(mean_yield = mean(yield)) |>
        summarize(diff = diff(mean_yield)) |>
        pull(diff)
})
head(simulated_diffs)

[1]  2.305714 -1.914286  4.940000  2.337143 -1.040000 -3.597143


Calculate p-value

p_value = (1 + sum(abs(simulated_diffs) >= abs(observed_diff))) / (nsim + 1)
p_value

[1] 0.03659634


Comments: Treatment a (the proposed better fertilizer) produced a mean yield 6.53 pounds higher than treatment b (p = 0.037). This provides moderate evidence against the null hypothesis of no fertilizer effect — a difference this large would occur by chance alone only about 3.7% of the time under random assignment. Since the 7 plants per group were randomly assigned to treatments, a causal conclusion is justified: the better fertilizer caused higher yield in this experiment. However, since the 14 plants were not randomly selected from any broader population, we cannot generalize beyond these particular plants.

title: Lab 01 — Introduction to R and the Creativity Study
text:
The one that I believe best represents the average weight of the population is 13.
5 rocks that I believe best represents the average weight of the population are 13, 7, 83, 81 and 62.
5 rocks that best represent variability are 67, 69, 13, 95 amd 84

title: Lab 01 — Introduction to R and the Creativity Study
text:
The one that I believe best represents the average weight of the population is 13.
5 rocks that I believe best represents the average weight of the population are 13, 7, 83, 81 and 62.
5 rocks that best represent variability are 67, 69, 13, 95 amd 84

title: Lab 01 — Introduction to R and the Creativity Study
text:
2. Introduction to Computing

Basics
Load necessary packages

library(dplyr)


Attaching package: 'dplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

library(ggplot2)

Read CSV

creativity <- read.csv("../data/creativity.csv")
names(creativity)

[1] "treatment" "score"    


Print the first 6 (default) observations

head(creativity)

  treatment score
1 intrinsic  12.0
2 intrinsic  12.0
3 intrinsic  12.9
4 intrinsic  13.6
5 intrinsic  16.6
6 intrinsic  17.2


Quick six-number summary of numerical column score

summary(creativity$score)

   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
   5.00   14.90   18.70   17.88   21.25   29.70 


Summarize all variables

summarize(creativity)

data frame with 0 columns and 1 row




Using the “pipe” operator |>

creativity |>
    head()

  treatment score
1 intrinsic  12.0
2 intrinsic  12.0
3 intrinsic  12.9
4 intrinsic  13.6
5 intrinsic  16.6
6 intrinsic  17.2


Select columns from a data frame

creativity |>
    select(score) |>
    head()

  score
1  12.0
2  12.0
3  12.9
4  13.6
5  16.6
6  17.2


Return six-number summary on the score column

creativity |>
    pull(score) |> # pull extracts a vector of the column values
    summary()

   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
   5.00   14.90   18.70   17.88   21.25   29.70 


Return the mean of the score column

creativity |>
    pull(score) |>
    mean()

[1] 17.8766


Return mean using summarize:

creativity |>
    summarize(
        meanScore = mean(score)
    )

  meanScore
1   17.8766


Summarize many expressions

creativity |>
    summarize(
        meanScore = mean(score),
        medianScore = median(score),
        sdScore = sd(score)
    )

  meanScore medianScore  sdScore
1   17.8766        18.7 5.246664


Calculate summaries by treatment group

creativity |>
    group_by(treatment) |>
    summarize(
        meanScore = mean(score),
        medianScore = median(score),
        sdScore = sd(score)
    )

# A tibble: 2 × 4
  treatment meanScore medianScore sdScore
  <chr>         <dbl>       <dbl>   <dbl>
1 extrinsic      15.8        17.2    5.29
2 intrinsic      19.9        20.4    4.44




Plotting Data
Create a dot plot

Center all dots on x-axis value 1
y is the score variable
binaxis is the axis to bin along, using y here
stackdir specifies direction in which dots are stacked


ggplot(data = creativity, mapping = aes(x = 1, y = score)) +
    geom_dotplot(binwidth = 1, binaxis = "y", stackdir = "center")








Create dot plots by treatment group

Use the treatment column in the x-axis


ggplot(data = creativity, mapping = aes(x = treatment, y = score)) +
    geom_dotplot(binwidth = 1, binaxis = "y", stackdir = "center")








Avoid redundancy by building a template and adding different geom layers to the template

Create a template


creativity_setup1 <- ggplot(data = creativity, mapping = aes(x = score))


Add geom layer - histogram


creativity_setup1 + geom_histogram()

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.










Add geom layer box_plot


creativity_setup1 + geom_boxplot()









Flip the box plot x and y axes


creativity_setup1 + 
    coord_flip() +
    geom_boxplot()








Work with treatment group

creativity_setup2 <- ggplot(data = creativity, mapping = aes(x = treatment, y = score))


Draw box plot per treatment group


creativity_setup2 + geom_boxplot()









Change theme to classic theme and redraw the same box plot


creativity_setup2 + 
    geom_boxplot() +
    theme_classic()








Specify different colors to visually separate groups

Create the template


creativity_setup3 <- ggplot(
    data = creativity, 
    mapping = aes(
        x = score,
        fill = treatment,
        color = treatment
    )
)


Draw histogram


creativity_setup3 + 
    geom_histogram(position = "dodge")

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.










Change color scale


creativity_setup3 +
    scale_color_grey() + 
    scale_fill_grey() +
    geom_histogram(position = "dodge")

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.









Save the last plot

ggsave("figure.png")

Saving 7 x 5 in image
`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.


Save a plot for HW1 submission:

creativity_setup2 + 
    geom_boxplot() +
    theme_classic()







ggsave("creativity_boxplot.png", width = 6, height = 4, dpi = 300)



Self-Assessment
Read tomato.csv

tomato_df <- read.csv("../data/tomato.csv")
names(tomato_df)

[1] "treatment" "yield"    


View the first 6 records

head(tomato_df)

  treatment yield
1         a  0.39
2         b  0.44
3         a  1.83
4         a  5.66
5         b  0.33
6         a  8.06


Draw side-by-side box plots of each yield for each of the two fertilizers

ggplot(data = tomato_df, mapping = aes(x = treatment, y = yield)) +
    geom_boxplot() +
    theme_classic()








Estimate the difference in mean yield between the two fertilizers (a - b). Include units.

mean_a = tomato_df |> filter(treatment == "a") |> pull(yield) |> mean()
mean_b = tomato_df |> filter(treatment == "b") |> pull(yield) |> mean()
paste("Mean difference: ", round(mean_a - mean_b, 2), "pounds")

[1] "Mean difference:  6.53 pounds"


Estimate the difference between in median yield the two fertilizers (a - b). Include units.

median_a = tomato_df |> filter(treatment == "a") |> pull(yield) |> median()
median_b = tomato_df |> filter(treatment == "b") |> pull(yield) |> median()
paste("Median difference: ", round(median_a - median_b, 2), "pounds")

[1] "Median difference:  4.64 pounds"
